@Fokko - To further add to Keliang explanation of separating the operator to kick off the job and sensor to monitor the job is to provide flexibility to users. Based on their use case(s), they may have the requirements to kick of multiple jobs/tasks in parallel and then monitor the completion of all Amazon Sagemaker job(s) downstream. Some these jobs may take hours and we don't want to hold the pipeline to initiate other downstream jobs hampering the users from meeting their required SLA. Since there are many other known and unknown scenarios, we are careful not to club both the initialization and monitoring of the job. 

The design is similar to the Amazon EMR - https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/contrib/example_dags/example_emr_job_flow_manual_steps.py

That's what I thought as well. Here is why I always add 1 to try number: https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/models.py#L1312. We can either make everything start from 0, including the name of log file and UI to display the attempt number, or add 1 to everything. Latter make more sense to user and former is better for the codebase.

How big can a request (dumps in line 179) and a response be? The loads/dumps API is inherently hostile towards asyncio. ```yaml
```yaml
a:
  $ref: '#/b'

  $ref: '#/c'

```

My rolling_volatility takes in the returns and the window as an integer. It returns a pd.Series.

Empyrical.annual_volatility also takes in the returns, but no window over which to compute the vol, since it does not return a pd.Series, but instead a float. Empyricals annual vol takes in a period as a string over which period to annualize, e.g., daily, weekly, ...

The only way I could use empyrical.annual_volatility in my function is, if I split the returns into sublists of length "rolling_vol_window" and compute empyrical.annual_volatility over each of those, which makes it really ugly and maybe also slow.

Hm, I think I like the idea of `chain_split` but haven't fallen in :heart: with any of these APIs. Maybe `chain_split` should actually return a tuple of chains (and so is always intended to be used last in the `pipe`):

```py
chaina, chainb = pipe(
    chain,
    mine_block(),
    mine_block(),
    chain_split(
        (mine_block(), ),  # fork A
        (mine_block(extra_data=b'fork-it!'), mine_block(), mine_block(), # fork B
    ),
)
```

---

FWIW, I think the current approach for forking is okay (explicit and clear). Maybe a slightly special version of `pipe` should always be used that never mutates the original chain. Then we wouldn't need a `fork_chain` at all, it would just be:

```py
    pre_fork_chain = build_chain(
        mining_chain,
        mine_block(),  # 1
        mine_block(),  # 2
        mine_block(),  # 3
    )

    fork_chain = build_chain(  # every build_chain automatically forks so the original isn't mutated
        pre_fork_chain,
        at_block_number(2),
        mine_block(extra_data=b'fork-it!'),  # fork 3
        mine_block(),  # fork 4
        mine_block(),  # fork 5
    )
```

I guess the above `chain_split` is easier to read.

Please remove otLogFunc* from OT core.

**PART 1**

First: These are never suggested as a replacement for, or use in normal 'release code'.
Developers should use the standard log functions when and where they are usable.

abtink>> If so, why not use the existing otLog<Level><Module>() APIs and just map otPlatLog to use a sperate debug/log UART (which may be separate channel/uart from what NCP uses)...

That is exactly what the code is doing here, just look at the call flow (below)

That said, the developer is free to use those platform level functions if they choose to.
perhaps  have made it more confusing by pointing out that somebody *can* use these.

**START HERE:**  The `otLog<Level><Module>()`  macro - in the DebugUart case ...

The macro expands to:  `otPlatDebugUart_otPlatLog()` 

https://github.com/DuaneEllis-TI/openthread/blob/debug-uart/src/core/utils/debug_uart.cpp#L107

That function ... 

Invokes: `otPlatDebugUart_printf()` and 
Invokes: `otPlatDebugUart_vprintf()` 
Invokes: `otPlatDebugUart_putchar()` for the terminal newline.

They in turn ... invokes others ... and they need prototypes

Example:   `otPlatDebugUart_puts_no_nl()` 

https://github.com/DuaneEllis-TI/openthread/blob/debug-uart/src/core/utils/debug_uart.cpp#L78

Example:  `otPlatDebugUart_putchar()` 

https://github.com/DuaneEllis-TI/openthread/blob/debug-uart/src/core/utils/debug_uart.cpp#L195

Example: `otPlatDebugUart_putchar_raw()`  a platform specific function

https://github.com/DuaneEllis-TI/openthread/blob/debug-uart/examples/platforms/cc2652/uart.c#L329

**PART 2**

But you asked about PROS & CONS of this?

Rather then have every platform implement the common stuff... 

Example, Nortic has a solution tied 100% to the SEGGER debugger, see this as an example:

https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/examples/platforms/nrf52840/logging.c

It would be better to refactor something like that and make it available to all platforms, that is what I am doing here.

**PART 3**

That said, we could hide those internal functions 

But by exposing these functions there are a few underlying features that are helpful here.

1) To keep the platform layer super simple, the best API at the platform specific layer 3 functions:  uart write byte,  uart kbhit, and uart get byte.  that's the minimal requirement for an platform.

This helps with the above quit a bit.

2) You'll notice that all of these functions are "weak" - for a reason.

For some platforms, and some debug situations - Forcing all debug to go through "putchar" is at times a horribly slow thing :-( but it is simple and works in the common case.

A better solution on Cortex platforms is the SWO trace feature - different vendors have different methods of exposing the feature, that's why there are a few layers of code exposed the silicon vendor can choose which one they want to connect at - by overriding the specific WEAK symbol.

This is sort of what Nortic is doing with the calls to the SEGGER debugger functions.

3) In the *NON*  log scheme - when you are debugging crashes - these functions are helpful in another way.

The standard LOG functions require and use printf() - In the embedded world there are times when for example, when printf() causes problems.  For example some versions of printf() require a heap (malloc/free) that is functional, or everything else has been scribbled on because of the bug  you are hunting down.

Technically to meet the goal of the debug uart the other functions are technically not required, but - they are exposed for the reasons above - there is nothing stopping somebody from using them - which leads me to the next reason.

4) Often when debugging something things "lock up" - and end at a while(1) loop. that's nice, but hard to debug. Even harder if the CPU ended up at the FaultISR vector.  even harder if the debugger was not attached and ready to go, sometimes plugging the debugger in will reset the board. GRRR!!!!

Think about a test farm with 30 devices running the compliance test suite

Why not convert the wile(1) loop into something that continuously dumps the cpu registers and the stack, all you need to do is connect the serial cable.

Take a look at this, this is the basis for CortexM parts

       http://www.freertos.org/Debugging-Hard-Faults-On-Cortex-M-Microcontrollers.html

The challenge here is this: You cannot use printf() because the infrastructure it requires is toast.  

Writing a simple print hex values routine is simple given these lower level functions, and won't take up much code

Here's an example from ARM7 tdmi in some stuff I wrote years ago.

Starting at the data abort handler (equal to the FAULT handler in CortexM series)

https://sourceforge.net/p/lostarm/code/HEAD/tree/trunk/chips/generic_arm/source/default_da_handler.c

Which calls a common crash handler:

https://sourceforge.net/p/lostarm/code/HEAD/tree/trunk/chips/generic_arm/source/common_xxx_handler.c

Which then dumps cpu registers

https://sourceforge.net/p/lostarm/code/HEAD/tree/trunk/chips/generic_arm/source/reg-dump.c

And a small stack dump (the ARM7tdmi provides a stack frame pointer, which makes printing a stack backtrace simple)

https://sourceforge.net/p/lostarm/code/HEAD/tree/trunk/chips/generic_arm/source/stackdump.c

-Duane.
